title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text: members, contributors, leaders pledge make participation community harassment-free experience everyone.

title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text: Examples behavior contributes positive environment: - Using welcoming inclusive language - respectful differing viewpoints experiences - Gracefully accepting constructive criticism - Focusing best community Examples unacceptable behavior: - Trolling, insulting derogatory comments, personal attacks - Public private harassment - Publishing others’ private information without explicit permission

title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text: Instances abusive, harassing, otherwise unacceptable behavior may reported project maintainers. complaints reviewed investigated promptly fairly.

title: Contributing to cjdiag
text: Fork repository Create feature branch (git checkout -b feature/-feature) Make changes Run devtools::check() — ensure 0 errors, 0 warnings Run devtools::test() — ensure tests pass Add tests new functionality Update NEWS.md Submit pull request

title: Contributing to cjdiag
text: Follow tidyverse style guide Use roxygen2 documentation exported functions need @param, @return, @examples

title: Contributing to cjdiag
text: Use testthat (edition 3) Test exported functions Keep tests fast (use small synthetic data, full datasets)

title: Getting Started with cjdiag
text: Standard conjoint analysis tools estimate Average Marginal Component Effects (AMCEs) — causal effect changing single attribute level. AMCEs tell respondents prefer, decide: attribute levels actually attend , ones ignore, order process information. cjdiag fills gap. works level individual attribute levels — aggregated attributes — specific level (e.g., “plans look work”, just “Job Plans” whole) triggers respondent decisions. use bundled immigration conjoint Hainmueller & Hopkins (2015): 2,000 profile evaluations, 9 attributes, ~50 attribute levels.
code: library(cjdiag) data(immig) f <- Chosen_Immigrant ~ Gender + Education + LanguageSkills +   CountryofOrigin + Job + JobExperience + JobPlans +   ReasonforApplication + PriorEntry

what: 1. Random Forest: Which attribute levels matter most?
title: Getting Started with cjdiag
text: Random forests measure much individual attribute level contributes predicting choices. Mean Decrease Accuracy (MDA) captures much worse predictions get level’s values shuffled — higher means important. root node rate tracks often level appears first split, proxy cue respondents check first.  top levels specific: “plans look work” (JobPlans), “formal” education, “without authorization” (PriorEntry), “fluent English”. attribute levels drive choices — attributes aggregated categories.
code: rf <- cj_fit(f, data = immig, method = "forest") rf #> Conjoint Random Forest  #> ======================  #>  #> Resolution: levels #> Trees: 500 #> OOB Error: 40.3% #> Observations: 2,000 #> Attributes: 9 #> Levels: 50 #>  #> Top 10 levels by MDA: #>  #> # A tibble: 10 × 5 #>     rank attribute       level                       mda root_pct #>    <int> <chr>           <chr>                     <dbl>    <dbl> #>  1     1 JobPlans        no plans to look for work 13.5      15.4 #>  2     2 JobPlans        contract with employer     8.18     11.2 #>  3     3 Education       no formal                  7.87      7.4 #>  4     4 PriorEntry      once w/o authorization     7.42     10.4 #>  5     5 LanguageSkills  fluent English             6.16      8.2 #>  6     6 PriorEntry      once as tourist            4.83      2.4 #>  7     7 Education       college degree             4.75      6.4 #>  8     8 LanguageSkills  used interpreter           4.66      5.6 #>  9     9 CountryofOrigin Iraq                       4.15      4.6 #> 10    10 Job             janitor                    3.87      3 plot(rf, top_n = 25)

what: 2. Decision Tree: How do respondents structure their decisions?
title: Getting Started with cjdiag
text: single classification tree reveals hierarchical elimination structure. root split gatekeeper — attribute level matters . Deeper splits conditional earlier ones. tree uses subset available levels, consistent respondents processing key cues rather information.
code: tr <- cj_fit(f, data = immig, method = "tree") tr #> Conjoint Decision Tree  #> ======================  #>  #> Resolution: levels #> Complexity (cp): 0.005 #> Root split: JobPlansno.plans.to.look.for.work #> Depth: 4 #> Terminal nodes: 6 #> Observations: 2,000 #> Levels: 50 #>  #> Top 10 levels by importance: #>  #> # A tibble: 10 × 4 #>     rank attribute      level                     importance #>    <int> <chr>          <chr>                          <dbl> #>  1     1 JobPlans       no plans to look for work      15.0  #>  2     2 PriorEntry     once w/o authorization          6.51 #>  3     3 Education      college degree                  3.81 #>  4     4 LanguageSkills used interpreter                3.49 #>  5     5 LanguageSkills fluent English                  3.21 #>  6     6 Gender         female                          0    #>  7     7 Gender         male                            0    #>  8     8 Education      4th grade                       0    #>  9     9 Education      8th grade                       0    #> 10    10 Education      graduate degree                 0 plot(tr)

what: 3. Nested Marginal Means: In what order do attribute levels settle choices?
title: Getting Started with cjdiag
text: Nested marginal means (Dill, Howlett & Mueller-Crepon 2024) work attribute levels sequentially. step, method identifies level whose marginal mean deviates 50/50 (decisive level), removes choice tasks level discriminate (profiles share ), repeats. cumulative plot shows quickly top levels account total decisiveness.
code: nmm <- cj_fit(f, data = immig, method = "nmm", resp_id = "CaseID", n_boot = 0) nmm #> Conjoint Nested Marginal Means  #> ==============================  #>  #> Observations: 2,000 #> Attributes: 9 #> Levels: 50 #>  #> Total pairs: 1,000 #> After top 5: 205 (20.5% remaining) #>  #> Top 10 levels by decisiveness: #>  #> # A tibble: 10 × 6 #>     rank attribute       level                      mm decisiveness pct_of_total #>    <int> <chr>           <chr>                   <dbl>        <dbl>        <dbl> #>  1     1 JobPlans        no plans to look for w… 0.305        0.389         38   #>  2     2 Education       college degree          0.687        0.375         16   #>  3     3 Education       no formal               0.331        0.339         12.1 #>  4     4 PriorEntry      once w/o authorization  0.303        0.395         11.9 #>  5     5 Job             computer programmer     0.733        0.467          1.5 #>  6     6 Job             doctor                  0.688        0.375          1.6 #>  7     7 CountryofOrigin Somalia                 0.714        0.429          2.1 #>  8     8 Education       graduate degree         0.712        0.423          5.2 #>  9     9 CountryofOrigin China                   0.762        0.524          2.1 #> 10    10 Job             nurse                   0.667        0.333          2.4 plot(nmm)

what: 4. Marginal R-squared: Which attributes did each respondent actually use?
title: Getting Started with cjdiag
text: individual respondent, marginal R-squared (Jenke et al. 2021) measures well attribute alone explains choices. Respondents R-squared near zero attribute likely ignored entirely. detects attribute non-attendance individual level.
code: mr2 <- cj_fit(f, data = immig, method = "marginal_r2", resp_id = "CaseID") #> Warning in summary.lm(fit): essentially perfect fit: summary may be unreliable #> Warning in summary.lm(fit): essentially perfect fit: summary may be unreliable #> Warning in summary.lm(fit): essentially perfect fit: summary may be unreliable #> Warning in summary.lm(fit): essentially perfect fit: summary may levels by mean absolute coefficient: #>  #> # A tibble: 10 × 7 #>     rank attribute       level      mean_coef mean_abs_coef sd_coef attr_mean_r2 #>    <int> <chr>           <chr>          <dbl> 0.650        0.194 #>  8     8 Job             doctor       0.126           0.540   0.670        0.194 #>  9     9 CountryofOrigin Somalia      0.0603          0.538   0.667        0.193 #> 10    10 CountryofOrigin Germany      0.0108          0.537   0.665        0.193

what: 5. CRT/HierNet: Which attribute levels genuinely drive choices?
title: Getting Started with cjdiag
text: CRT applies increasing statistical penalty (L1 regularization via hierNet) progressively strip away weak signals. Levels keep effect even heavy penalization genuinely drive choices; levels vanish quickly noise redundant. Requires hierNet package.
code: crt <- cj_fit(f, data = immig, method = "crt",               lambda_grid = c(5, 10, 20, 50), n_folds = 3, n_perm = 5) crt #> Conjoint CRT/HierNet Model  #> ==========================  #>  #> Optimal lambda: 5 #> Lambda (1-SE rule): 5 #> Accuracy: 64.8% #> Observations: 2,000 #> Attributes: 9 #> Levels: 50 #> Attended levels: 50 / 50 #>  #> Top 10 levels by MDA: #>  #> # A tibble: 10 × 5 #>     rank attribute            level                       mda max_lambda #>    <int> <chr>                <chr>                     <dbl>      <dbl> #>  1     1 JobPlans             no plans to look for work 4.14          50 #>  2     2 JobPlans             contract with employer    3.49          50 #>  3     3 PriorEntry           once w/o authorization    1.88          50 #>  4     4 Education            college degree            1.81          50 #>  5     5 CountryofOrigin      Iraq                      1.77          50 #>  6     6 Education            no formal                 1.57          50 #>  7     7 LanguageSkills       used interpreter          1.28          50 #>  8     8 ReasonforApplication escape persecution        1.27          50 #>  9     9 LanguageSkills       fluent English            1.27          50 #> 10    10 ReasonforApplication seek better job           0.910         20

title: Getting Started with cjdiag
text: plot methods return ggplot2 objects accept customization:  Three palettes: "default", "colorblind" (Okabe-Ito), "grey". Set global defaults set_cjdiag_theme() set_cjdiag_labels() plots use settings without repeating arguments.
code: plot(rf,      palette = "colorblind",      attribute.names = c(LanguageSkills = "English Proficiency",                          JobPlans = "Plans for Employment"),      top_n = 20)

what: Diagnostic Tools for Conjoint Survey Experiments
title: Diagnostic Tools for Conjoint Survey Experiments
text: Tools attribute-level importance attendance conjoint survey experiments — attribute levels drive choices, rank, ones respondents ignore.

title: Diagnostic Tools for Conjoint Survey Experiments
text: Standard conjoint analysis tools (cjoint, cregg) estimate respondents prefer — Average Marginal Component Effects (AMCEs) marginal means. tell respondents decide: attributes actually attend , whether process information hierarchically, attribute levels ignore entirely. cjdiag fills gap five diagnostic methods reveal decision process behind conjoint choices.

title: Diagnostic Tools for Conjoint Survey Experiments
code: # Install from GitHub (CRAN submission pending) # install.packages("pak") pak::pak("dkarpa/cjdiag")

title: Diagnostic Tools for Conjoint Survey Experiments
code: library(cjdiag) data(immig)  rf <- cj_fit(   Chosen_Immigrant ~ Gender + Education + LanguageSkills +     CountryofOrigin + Job + JobExperience + JobPlans +     ReasonforApplication + PriorEntry,   data = immig,   method = "forest" )  rf #> Conjoint Random Forest  #> ======================  #>  #> Resolution: levels #> Trees: 500 #> OOB Error: 40.3% #> Observations: 2,000 #> Attributes: 9 #> Levels: 50 #>  #> Top 10 levels by MDA: #>  #> # A tibble: 10 × 5 #>     rank attribute       level                       mda root_pct #>    <int> <chr>           <chr>                     <dbl>    <dbl> #>  1     1 JobPlans        no plans to look for work 13.5      15.4 #>  2     2 JobPlans        contract with employer     8.18     11.2 #>  3     3 Education       no formal                  7.87      7.4 #>  4     4 PriorEntry      once w/o authorization     7.42     10.4 #>  5     5 LanguageSkills  fluent English             6.16      8.2 #>  6     6 PriorEntry      once as tourist            4.83      2.4 #>  7     7 Education       college degree             4.75      6.4 #>  8     8 LanguageSkills  used interpreter           4.66      5.6 #>  9     9 CountryofOrigin Iraq                       4.15      4.6 #> 10    10 Job             janitor                    3.87      3

title: Diagnostic Tools for Conjoint Survey Experiments
text: methods accessed single function: cj_fit(formula, data, method).

title: Diagnostic Tools for Conjoint Survey Experiments
text: plot methods return ggplot2 objects accept customization parameters: Three palettes available: "default", "colorblind" (Okabe-Ito), "grey". Set defaults set_cjdiag_theme() set_cjdiag_labels().
code: # Colorblind-safe palette plot(rf, palette = "colorblind")  # Rename attributes in display plot(rf, attribute.names = c(LanguageSkills = "English Proficiency"))  # Full ggplot2 theme override plot(rf, theme = ggplot2::theme_classic(base_size = 14))

title: Diagnostic Tools for Conjoint Survey Experiments
text: cjdiag complementary packages estimate AMCEs design conjoint experiments: cjoint — AMCE estimation (Hainmueller, Hopkins & Yamamoto) cregg — AMCE marginal means tidy output projoint — full conjoint pipeline cbcTools — conjoint experiment design power analysis Run cjoint cregg AMCEs, cjdiag diagnose respondents actually made choices.

title: Diagnostic Tools for Conjoint Survey Experiments
text: full walkthrough, see Introduction vignette.

what: Augment Data with Swapped Profile Order — augment_profile_order
title: Augment Data with Swapped Profile Order — augment_profile_order
text: Doubles dataset swapping left/right profiles inverting outcome. satisfies profile order constraint required valid CRT hypothesis testing HierNet. Apply calling cj_fit() method = "crt".

title: Augment Data with Swapped Profile Order — augment_profile_order
code: augment_profile_order(data, outcome, left, right)

title: Augment Data with Swapped Profile Order — augment_profile_order
text: data data frame containing conjoint data. outcome Character name binary outcome column (0/1). left Character vector column names left profile attributes. right Character vector column names right profile attributes. Must length left.

title: Augment Data with Swapped Profile Order — augment_profile_order
text: data frame 2 * nrow(data) rows: original data followed swapped copy (left/right exchanged, outcome inverted).

title: Augment Data with Swapped Profile Order — augment_profile_order
code: # \donttest{ df <- data.frame(   y = sample(0:1, 100, TRUE),   left_a = factor(sample(c("x","y"), 100, TRUE)),   left_b = factor(sample(c("p","q"), 100, TRUE)),   right_a = factor(sample(c("x","y"), 100, TRUE)),   right_b = factor(sample(c("p","q"), 100, TRUE)) ) augmented <- augment_profile_order(df, "y",   left = c("left_a", "left_b"), right = c("right_a", "right_b")) nrow(augmented)  # doubled #> [1] 200 # }

title: Fit Conjoint Diagnostic Model — cj_fit
text: Fits random forest decision tree model conjoint data. Use resolution = "levels" (default) level-specific analysis attribute level becomes separate binary predictor, resolution = "attributes" attribute-level analysis original factor columns passed directly model.

title: Fit Conjoint Diagnostic Model — cj_fit
code: cj_fit(   formula,   data,   method = c("forest", "tree", "crt", "nmm", "marginal_r2"),   resolution = c("levels", "attributes"),   ntree = 500L,   cp = 0.005,   lambda_grid = c(1, 2, 3, 5, 7, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 40, 50, 75, 100, 150, 200, 300, 400,     500),   n_folds = 5L,   n_perm = 20L,   tol = 0.001,   resp_id = NULL,   n_boot = 0L,   seed = 42L,   ... )

title: Fit Conjoint Diagnostic Model — cj_fit
text: formula formula form choice ~ attr1 + attr2 + ... outcome binary (0/1 2-level factor) predictors categorical attributes (converted factors internally). data data frame containing conjoint data. method Model type: "forest" (default), "tree", "crt", "nmm", "marginal_r2". resolution Analysis resolution: "levels" (default) level-specific dummy-coded analysis, "attributes" attribute-level analysis using original factors. ntree Number trees random forest (default 500). Ignored method = "tree". cp Complexity parameter decision tree (default 0.005). Ignored method = "forest". lambda_grid Numeric vector lambda values CRT regularization path (default c(1, 2, 3, 5, 7, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 40, 50, 75, 100, 150, 200, 300, 400, 500)). Ignored method "crt". n_folds Number cross-validation folds CRT (default 5). Ignored method "crt". n_perm Number permutation rounds CRT importance (default 20). Ignored method "crt". tol Convergence tolerance HierNet (default 1e-3). Ignored method "crt". resp_id Character string naming respondent ID column. Required method = "nmm" "marginal_r2". Ignored methods. n_boot Number bootstrap iterations NMM confidence intervals (default 0 = bootstrap). Ignored method "nmm". seed Random seed reproducibility (default 42). ... Additional arguments passed randomForest::randomForest() rpart::rpart().

title: Fit Conjoint Diagnostic Model — cj_fit
text: S3 object inheriting cjdiag_fit, subclass depending method (e.g., cjdiag_forest, cjdiag_tree, etc.). objects support print(), plot(), summary(), importance().

title: Fit Conjoint Diagnostic Model — cj_fit
text: "forest" (Random Forest) attributes matter choices? Measures much attribute level matters shuffling values checking much worse predictions get (Mean Decrease Accuracy). Also tracks attribute appears first across hundreds trees — proxy cue respondents check first. Supports level attribute resolution. "tree" (Decision Tree) respondents structure decisions? Fits single classification tree reveals hierarchical structure choices — attribute acts gatekeeper, attributes matter conditionally, many attributes needed explain choices. Supports resolutions. "crt" (CRT/HierNet) attribute levels genuinely drive choices? Applies increasing amounts statistical penalty strip away weak signals (Bien Tibshirani 2014). Levels keep effect even heavy penalization genuinely drive choices; levels vanish quickly noise redundant. Levels . "nmm" (Nested Marginal Means) order attributes settle choices? Works attributes one time, starting decisive (Dill, Howlett Mueller-Crepon 2024). step, identifies attribute level strongly tips choices away 50/50, removes tasks level discriminate, repeats. Requires resp_id. Levels . "marginal_r2" (Marginal R-squared) attributes respondent actually use? individual respondent, measures well attribute alone explains choices (Jenke, Bansak, Hainmueller Hangartner 2021). Respondents zero explanatory power attribute likely ignored entirely. Requires resp_id.

title: Fit Conjoint Diagnostic Model — cj_fit
code: # \donttest{ data(immig) rf <- cj_fit(Chosen_Immigrant ~ Gender + Education + LanguageSkills +              Job + JobPlans, data = immig, method = "forest") print(rf) #> Conjoint Random Forest  #> ======================  #>  #> Resolution: levels #> Trees: 500 #> OOB Error: 42.2% #> Observations: 2,000 #> Attributes: 5 #> Levels: 28 #>  #> Top 10 levels by MDA: #>  #> # A tibble: 10 × 5 #>     rank attribute      level                       mda root_pct #>    <int> <chr>          <chr>                     <dbl>    <dbl> #>  1     1 JobPlans       no plans to look for work 16.7      17.4 #>  2     2 LanguageSkills fluent English            12.3      11.2 #>  3     3 Education      no formal                 12.0       8.8 #>  4     4 LanguageSkills used interpreter          11.5       8.4 #>  5     5 JobPlans       contract with employer    10.3      13.2 #>  6     6 Education      college degree             7.84      9   #>  7     7 Job            janitor                    5.55      6.8 #>  8     8 Job            doctor                     4.50      3.6 #>  9     9 Education      graduate degree            3.27      2   #> 10    10 Job            gardener                   2.74      0.6 plot(rf)  summary(rf) #> Conjoint Random Forest Summary #>   500 trees, OOB error 42.2%, 2,000 obs, 28 levels #>   Top-1 MDA: 16.7 (30% of total) #>   Top-3 MDA: 41.0 (74% of total) #>   Root split: no plans to look for work (17.4%)  tr <- cj_fit(Chosen_Immigrant ~ Gender + Education + LanguageSkills +              Job + JobPlans, data = immig, method = "tree") plot(tr, type = "importance")  # }

title: Resolve Plot Options (internal) — .resolve_plot_options
text: Three-tier priority: explicit arg > global options > hardcoded default.

title: Immigration Conjoint Example Data — immig
text: Hainmueller & Hopkins (2015) immigration conjoint experiment. Contains ~1400 respondents, evaluating 5 pairs immigrant profiles (13,960 rows total). Nine attributes ~50 levels, binary forced-choice.

title: Immigration Conjoint Example Data — immig
code: immig

title: Immigration Conjoint Example Data — immig
text: data frame 13,960 rows 16 columns: CaseID Respondent identifier contest_no Task number (1-5) profile Profile number within task (1 2) Gender Immigrant gender: female, male Education Education level (7 levels: formal graduate degree) LanguageSkills English ability (4 levels) CountryofOrigin Country origin (10 levels) Job Occupation (11 levels) JobExperience Work experience (4 levels) JobPlans Employment plans (4 levels) ReasonforApplication Immigration reason (3 levels) PriorEntry Prior US entry history (5 levels) Chosen_Immigrant Binary outcome: 1 chosen, 0

title: Immigration Conjoint Example Data — immig
text: Hainmueller, J. & Hopkins, D. (2015). Hidden American Immigration Consensus: Conjoint Analysis Attitudes toward Immigrants. American Journal Political Science, 59(3), 529-548.

title: Immigration Conjoint Example Data — immig
code: data(immig) head(immig) #>   CaseID contest_no profile Gender       Education           LanguageSkills #> 1      4          1       1   male     high school tried English but unable #> 2      4          1       2 female       no formal         used interpreter #> 3      4          2       1 female graduate degree           fluent English #> 4      4          2       2 female       4th grade           fluent English #> 5      4          3       1 female     high school           broken English #> 6      4          3       2   male  college degree           fluent English #>   CountryofOrigin                 Job JobExperience                 JobPlans #> 1            Iraq               nurse      5+ years   contract with employer #> 2          France child care provider     3-5 years interviews with employer #> 3           Sudan            gardener     3-5 years   contract with employer #> 4         Germany construction worker      5+ years interviews with employer #> 5     Philippines               nurse      5+ years interviews with employer #> 6           Sudan child care provider          none   contract with employer #>   ReasonforApplication             PriorEntry Chosen_Immigrant #> 1      seek better job        once as tourist                1 #> 2      seek better job once w/o authorization                0 #> 3   escape persecution        once as tourist                0 #> 4  reunite with family                  never                1 #> 5      seek better job once w/o authorization                1 #> 6      seek better job                  never                0 table(immig$Chosen_Immigrant) #>  #>    0    1  #> 1000 1000

what: Extract Importance Metrics from Fitted Model — importance
title: Extract Importance Metrics from Fitted Model — importance
text: Extracts importance metrics fitted conjoint diagnostic model. Returns results whatever resolution model fitted : level-specific (resolution = "levels") attribute-level (resolution = "attributes").

title: Extract Importance Metrics from Fitted Model — importance
code: importance(x, ...)

title: Extract Importance Metrics from Fitted Model — importance
text: x fitted model object cj_fit() ... Additional arguments (unused)

title: Extract Importance Metrics from Fitted Model — importance
text: cjdiag_importance object (list) containing: results Tibble importance metrics fitted resolution method Character: "forest" "tree" resolution Character: "levels" "attributes"

title: Extract Importance Metrics from Fitted Model — importance
code: # \donttest{ df <- data.frame(   y = sample(0:1, 200, TRUE),   a = factor(sample(c("x","y"), 200, TRUE)),   b = factor(sample(c("p","q","r"), 200, TRUE)) ) rf <- cj_fit(y ~ a + b, data = df, method = "forest") imp <- importance(rf) print(imp) #> Conjoint Importance Metrics  #> ===========================  #>  #> Resolution: levels #> Method: Random Forest (500 trees) #> OOB Error: 56.5% #>  #> Level Importance (top 5 ): #>  #> # A tibble: 5 × 9 #>    rank attribute level   mda   mdg root_pct class_0 class_1 var_name #>   <int> <chr>     <chr> <dbl> <dbl>    <dbl>   <dbl>   <dbl> <chr>    #> 1     1 b         q     10.7  2.05      38.8  5.03     8.87  bq       #> 2     2 b         r      3.14 1.14      23.4  5.35    -2.10  br       #> 3     3 a         y      1.37 0.876     15   -0.0745   2.05  ay       #> 4     4 a         x     -1.03 1.03      11.4 -1.53    -0.178 ax       #> 5     5 b         p     -2.90 0.740     11.4 -0.398   -2.80  bp       as.data.frame(imp) #>   rank attribute level       mda       mdg root_pct     class_0   class_1 #> 1    1         b     q 10.698377 2.0492472     38.8  5.02811272  8.865870 #> 2    2         b     r  3.137717 1.1412145     23.4  5.34772910 -2.101405 #> 3    3         a     y  1.366344 0.8759098     15.0 -0.07452591  2.053108 #> 4    4         a     x -1.032809 1.0286025     11.4 -1.52541963 -0.177525 #> 5    5         b     p -2.902232 0.7395296     11.4 -0.39782900 -2.803436 #>   var_name #> 1       bq #> 2       br #> 3       ay #> 4       ax #> 5       bp # }

Additional arguments passed primary ggplot2 geom

title: Plot CRT/HierNet Results — plot.cjdiag_crt
text: ggplot object

title: Plot CRT/HierNet Results — plot.cjdiag_crt
code: # \donttest{ # CRT requires the hierNet package if (requireNamespace("hierNet", quietly = TRUE)) {   df <- data.frame(     y = sample(0:1, 200, TRUE),     a = factor(sample(c("x","y"), 200, TRUE)),     b = factor(sample(c("p","q","r"), 200, TRUE))   )   crt <- cj_fit(y ~ a + b, data = df, method = "crt",                  lambda_grid = c(5, 10), n_folds = 2, n_perm = 2)   plot(crt, type = "robustness") }  # }

title: Plot Nested Marginal Means Results — plot.cjdiag_nmm
code: # \donttest{ df <- data.frame(   y = sample(0:1, 200, TRUE),   a = factor(sample(c("x","y"), 200, TRUE)),   b = factor(sample(c("p","q","r"), 200, TRUE)),   id = rep(1:100, each = 2) ) nmm <- cj_fit(y ~ a + b, data = df, method = "nmm", resp_id = "id") plot(nmm, type = "decisiveness")  plot(nmm, type = "ranking", draw = FALSE)  # returns tibble #> Error in match.arg(type, c("cumulative", "decisiveness", "sample")): 'arg' should be one of “cumulative”, “decisiveness”, “sample” # }

code: set_cjdiag_theme(   base_size = 12,   palette = "default",   font_family = "",   label_wrap = 35L,   theme = NULL,   print_n = 10L )

title: Set Global Plot Theme Options — set_cjdiag_theme
text: base_size Default font size plots (default 12) palette Default color palette: "default", "colorblind", "grey" (default "default") font_family Default font family (default "") label_wrap Default character width label wrapping (default 35) theme complete ggplot2::theme() object use default (default NULL, uses theme_cjdiag()) print_n Default number rows shown print() methods (default 10)

title: Set Global Plot Theme Options — set_cjdiag_theme
text: Invisibly returns previous options (save/restore pattern)

what: Custom ggplot2 Theme for cjdiag — theme_cjdiag
title: Custom ggplot2 Theme for cjdiag — theme_cjdiag
text: publication-ready theme based ggplot2::theme_minimal() consistent defaults used across cjdiag plots. Removes minor gridlines, controls major gridlines, positions legend top.

title: Custom ggplot2 Theme for cjdiag — theme_cjdiag
text: base_size Base font size (default 12) base_family Base font family (default "") grid_y Show horizontal gridlines (default FALSE) grid_x Show vertical gridlines (default TRUE)

title: cjdiag 0.2.0
text: Initial CRAN release.

title: cjdiag 0.2.0
text: Random forest (method = "forest"): attribute importance via Mean Decrease Accuracy, Mean Decrease Gini, root node appearance rates. Supports level-specific attribute-level resolution. Decision tree (method = "tree"): CART classification tree revealing hierarchical decision structure. Root split identifies gatekeeper attribute. CRT/HierNet (method = "crt"): L1-regularized logistic regression across lambda grid. Lambda path, cross-validation, permutation importance (MDA). Nested marginal means (method = "nmm"): sequential elimination procedure. Decisiveness ranking reveals decision order. Marginal R-squared (method = "marginal_r2"): per-respondent per-attribute importance metric.

title: cjdiag 0.2.0
text: Unified cj_fit(formula, data, method) interface methods. importance() generic methods model types. Plot customization: palette, base_size, label_wrap, attribute.names, level.names, group_by_attribute, full theme override. Three palettes: "default", "colorblind" (Okabe-Ito), "grey". Global options: set_cjdiag_theme(), set_cjdiag_labels(), get_cjdiag_options(). tidy() glance() methods (conditional broom/generics). Bundled immig dataset (Hainmueller & Hopkins 2015).
